name: Authentication and Login Security — additive questions
description: Question expansion derived from the attached source files; reuses existing repository topic IDs and defines only missing concepts.

topics.name: OIDC Authorization Code Flow with PKCE
topics.description: Identity login with OpenID Connect and authorization-code interception protection through PKCE.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

OpenID Connect adds identity claims and an ID token to OAuth 2.0, while PKCE protects the authorization code flow for clients that cannot keep a secret. They solve different problems and are often used together.

OIDC answers who the user is, and PKCE proves that the client exchanging an authorization code is the same client that started the flow.

## What problem does it solve?

Using an access token as proof of identity can mix authorization and login semantics. Without PKCE, a stolen authorization code from a mobile or browser app may be exchanged by another process.

## How it works

A single-page app creates a random code verifier and sends only its hash as the code challenge. After login it exchanges the returned code together with the original verifier and validates the OIDC ID token.

- Request the openid scope when the application needs login identity
- Validate the ID token's signature, issuer, audience, nonce, and time claims
- Create a high-entropy PKCE verifier and send its derived challenge
- Present the verifier during code exchange so a stolen code alone is useless

## Common mistakes

- Calling an access token and ID token interchangeable
- Treating PKCE as user authentication or token encryption
- Using a predictable verifier or failing to bind nonce to the login request

## Key terms

- ID token — an OIDC token containing claims about the authenticated user
- PKCE — a proof that binds code exchange to the client that started the flow
- nonce — a value that binds an ID token to one login request

questions.title: Applying Secure Cookie Attributes in practice
questions.prompt: A session cookie is set with HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax, a narrow Path, and a short Max-Age. JavaScript cannot read it, plain HTTP cannot carry it, and most cross-site requests do not include it. What would you validate or change before approving it, and how would you test the protections?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains HttpOnly
expectedPoints.description: States that JavaScript cannot read the cookie and connects this to token theft through XSS.

expectedPoints.label: Explains Secure and SameSite
expectedPoints.description: Connects Secure to HTTPS and SameSite to cross-site request behavior and CSRF reduction.

expectedPoints.label: Limits scope and lifetime
expectedPoints.description: Mentions narrow Domain or Path and an appropriate Max-Age or expiry rather than permanent broad cookies.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Secure Cookie Attributes
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Believing HttpOnly prevents every effect of XSS." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains HttpOnly
expectedPoints.description: States that JavaScript cannot read the cookie and connects this to token theft through XSS.

expectedPoints.label: Explains Secure and SameSite
expectedPoints.description: Connects Secure to HTTPS and SameSite to cross-site request behavior and CSRF reduction.

expectedPoints.label: Limits scope and lifetime
expectedPoints.description: Mentions narrow Domain or Path and an appropriate Max-Age or expiry rather than permanent broad cookies.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Using SameSite=None without Secure?

questions.title: Applying Session vs Token Authentication in practice
questions.prompt: A web app stores only an opaque session ID in a cookie and keeps the session in Redis. A separate mobile API uses short-lived access tokens and rotates refresh tokens because many services must validate requests independently. What would you validate or change before approving it, and how would you test the protections?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains where state lives
expectedPoints.description: Contrasts an opaque session ID with authoritative server-side state against a token that carries signed claims.

expectedPoints.label: Compares operational behavior
expectedPoints.description: Discusses session-store availability and token expiry, key distribution, logout, and revocation.

expectedPoints.label: Makes a contextual choice
expectedPoints.description: Selects based on clients, service boundaries, security needs, and operational capabilities rather than a universal rule.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Session vs Token Authentication
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Calling JWT automatically more secure or more scalable." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains where state lives
expectedPoints.description: Contrasts an opaque session ID with authoritative server-side state against a token that carries signed claims.

expectedPoints.label: Compares operational behavior
expectedPoints.description: Discusses session-store availability and token expiry, key distribution, logout, and revocation.

expectedPoints.label: Makes a contextual choice
expectedPoints.description: Selects based on clients, service boundaries, security needs, and operational capabilities rather than a universal rule.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Putting sensitive data into a readable token payload?

questions.title: Applying JWT Structure and Validation in practice
questions.prompt: An API receives a bearer token, verifies its signature with the provider's current public key, checks iss and aud, rejects expired tokens, and only then uses the subject and roles. What would you validate or change before approving it, and how would you test the protections?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes decoding from verification
expectedPoints.description: Explains that Base64 decoding reveals untrusted data, while verification proves integrity and signer authenticity.

expectedPoints.label: Validates required claims
expectedPoints.description: Checks issuer, audience, expiry, not-before, and optionally token type or nonce according to the use case.

expectedPoints.label: Restricts algorithms and keys
expectedPoints.description: Uses an allow-list of algorithms and the correct trusted key set, including safe key rotation.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about JWT Structure and Validation
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Saying that Base64 encoding hides the payload." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes decoding from verification
expectedPoints.description: Explains that Base64 decoding reveals untrusted data, while verification proves integrity and signer authenticity.

expectedPoints.label: Validates required claims
expectedPoints.description: Checks issuer, audience, expiry, not-before, and optionally token type or nonce according to the use case.

expectedPoints.label: Restricts algorithms and keys
expectedPoints.description: Uses an allow-list of algorithms and the correct trusted key set, including safe key rotation.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Using decoded claims before verifying the signature?

questions.title: Applying OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow in practice
questions.prompt: A user chooses Sign in with Provider. The browser returns to the application's redirect URI with a short-lived code and state value; the backend validates state and exchanges the code at the provider's token endpoint. What would you validate or change before approving it, and how would you test the protections?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the complete flow
expectedPoints.description: Covers redirect to authorization, user consent, code return, and token exchange at the token endpoint.

expectedPoints.label: Explains state and redirect validation
expectedPoints.description: Connects state to request correlation and CSRF defense, and requires an exact registered redirect URI.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the back channel
expectedPoints.description: States why tokens are obtained through a protected server-to-server or app-to-provider exchange instead of a browser URL.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Describing OAuth as a login protocol by itself." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the complete flow
expectedPoints.description: Covers redirect to authorization, user consent, code return, and token exchange at the token endpoint.

expectedPoints.label: Explains state and redirect validation
expectedPoints.description: Connects state to request correlation and CSRF defense, and requires an exact registered redirect URI.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the back channel
expectedPoints.description: States why tokens are obtained through a protected server-to-server or app-to-provider exchange instead of a browser URL.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Sending a client secret from a browser or mobile app?

questions.title: OAuth, OIDC, and PKCE
questions.prompt: Explain the difference between OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and PKCE in a modern browser or mobile login.
questions.answerStructureHint: State the problem each solves, then describe ID token validation and the verifier/challenge exchange.

expectedPoints.label: Separates OAuth and OIDC
expectedPoints.description: Explains OAuth as delegated authorization and OIDC as an identity layer that provides an ID token.

expectedPoints.label: Explains PKCE
expectedPoints.description: Describes code verifier and challenge and how they protect public clients from authorization-code interception.

expectedPoints.label: Explains login validation
expectedPoints.description: Mentions signature, issuer, audience, nonce, and time checks before trusting the ID token.

followUps.prompt: Why should an API normally receive an access token rather than an ID token?

questions.title: Applying Refresh Tokens and Rotation in practice
questions.prompt: A mobile app exchanges refresh token R1 and receives a new access token plus R2. R1 becomes invalid; if R1 appears again, the server treats it as possible theft and revokes the token family. What would you validate or change before approving it, and how would you test the protections?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains different lifetimes
expectedPoints.description: Connects short access-token lifetime to reduced theft impact and refresh tokens to session continuity.

expectedPoints.label: Explains rotation
expectedPoints.description: States that each use invalidates the old refresh token and issues a new one.

expectedPoints.label: Handles token reuse
expectedPoints.description: Treats reuse as a theft signal and revokes the family or session, with appropriate user reauthentication.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Refresh Tokens and Rotation
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Rotating access tokens but reusing one refresh token forever." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains different lifetimes
expectedPoints.description: Connects short access-token lifetime to reduced theft impact and refresh tokens to session continuity.

expectedPoints.label: Explains rotation
expectedPoints.description: States that each use invalidates the old refresh token and issues a new one.

expectedPoints.label: Handles token reuse
expectedPoints.description: Treats reuse as a theft signal and revokes the family or session, with appropriate user reauthentication.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Sending refresh tokens to every API endpoint?

questions.title: Applying Authentication Threat Model in practice
questions.prompt: A service protects login with rate limits and generic errors, rotates the session after authentication, stores credentials in protected cookies, validates OAuth state, and invalidates sessions after password reset. What would you validate or change before approving it, and how would you test the protections?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Covers browser and network threats
expectedPoints.description: Mentions HTTPS, secure cookie or token storage, XSS, CSRF, session fixation, and redirect validation.

expectedPoints.label: Covers credentials and abuse
expectedPoints.description: Includes strong password hashing, rate limiting, credential-stuffing defense, generic errors, and optional MFA.

expectedPoints.label: Covers lifecycle and recovery
expectedPoints.description: Discusses expiry, revocation, logout, refresh rotation, password reset, recovery, and security monitoring.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Authentication Threat Model
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Treating CORS as CSRF protection." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Covers browser and network threats
expectedPoints.description: Mentions HTTPS, secure cookie or token storage, XSS, CSRF, session fixation, and redirect validation.

expectedPoints.label: Covers credentials and abuse
expectedPoints.description: Includes strong password hashing, rate limiting, credential-stuffing defense, generic errors, and optional MFA.

expectedPoints.label: Covers lifecycle and recovery
expectedPoints.description: Discusses expiry, revocation, logout, refresh rotation, password reset, recovery, and security monitoring.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Storing passwords with reversible encryption?
